title: Opponent-aware undercut/overcut counterfactual features
summary: F1 strategy references consistently frame pit timing as an interaction problem driven by traffic, out-lap delta, and the gap to the nearest pit-window rival, while game-theoretic work models each lap’s stop/continue choice as dependent on the opponent’s action.
rationale: F1 strategy references consistently frame pit timing as an interaction problem driven by traffic, out-lap delta, and the gap to the nearest pit-window rival, while game-theoretic work models each lap’s stop/continue choice as dependent on the opponent’s action. The current best code is mostly self-state plus field averages; it appears to lack explicit nearest-rival pit-window economics.
implementation_hint: Within each Year-Race-LapNumber, compute gaps to nearest cars ahead/behind in Position or lap time, then add counterfactual urgency features such as expected net gain from pitting now vs staying out one lap: LapTime_Delta relative to nearby rivals, tyre-age differential vs nearest rival, same-compound rival old-tyre flags, and clean-air proxies after a hypothetical stop. Validate with GroupKFold/leave-race-out so same-race future rows never inform aggregates.
expected_effect: Could add orthogonal signal near ambiguous pit windows where self-tyre features alone cannot separate cover-the-rival stops from delayed stops.
risk: Easy to leak same-lap or future race information if rival aggregates use future rows; counterfactual features may be noisy without exact gap telemetry.
